other_observers: Glen Walbek; Cheryl Touten found the bird earlier in the day. The bird was supposedly first I.D.'ed as a Pomerine Jaeger.
description: The head pattern combined with the long tail streamers make this a jaeger. The tail streamers were dark gray, very long and flexible (photo), the length of them being greater than the greatest width of the wings. The face was white with a faint yellowish cast. The crown was dark contrasting stongly with the whitish face. The bill was short and dark.  In flight and from the dorsal view, the wings showed what I thought were two silver outer primary shafts possibly three. The underwings were uniform browinish with no light-colored crescents at the base of the primaries. The breast was white and stood out most when the bird was floating on the water. The bird seemed very buoyant, sometimes only the breast seemed to be touching the water and the tail was elevated at a 25 degree angle.Behaviors: The jaeger was viewed floating in calm water and also it chased California Gulls and also was chased by them. The chasing was infrequent and did not seem to be very aggressive on the part of the jaeger. It flew a lot to different parts of the reservoir. In flight the jaeger never flew more than 40 feet above the water.Call: nonePlumage: adult
photographer: Photographer: bill maynard, bill maynard, bill maynard, bill maynard
